questions.stem: Which of the following statements about bacteria is correct?

options.t: Generally, they can replicate independently outside host cells
They contain both DNA and RNA

questions.explanation: Bacteria are cellular, replicate independently (unlike viruses), and contain both DNA and RNA — all true.

questions.stem: In what size range can all known bacteria be placed?
questions.explanation: Bacteria span roughly 0.2 µm to ~1000 µm.

questions.stem: Bacteria can be identified:

options.t: Based on morphological and staining characteristics

questions.explanation: Identification uses morphology/staining, biochemistry and phylogenetics.

questions.stem: The 5-Kingdom classification system was developed by:
questions.explanation: Robert Whittaker proposed the 5-Kingdom system.

questions.stem: Which sequence best represents the hierarchy in biological classification?

options.t: Kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species
Kingdom, class, phylum, family, order, genus, species
Phylum, kingdom, order, class, family, species, genus
Kingdom, order, phylum, class, family, genus, species

questions.explanation: Kingdom → phylum → class → order → family → genus → species.

questions.stem: Gram staining is used for the identification of:

options.t: All bacteria, with a few exceptions

questions.explanation: Gram stain works for most bacteria, with exceptions (mycobacteria, mycoplasmas, spirochetes, chlamydiae).

questions.stem: Ziehl-Neelsen staining is used for the identification of:

options.t: All bacteria, with a few exceptions

questions.explanation: Ziehl-Neelsen (acid-fast) staining identifies mycobacteria.

questions.stem: The absence of a cell wall is a characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Mycoplasmas lack a cell wall (no peptidoglycan).

questions.stem: In Gram-negative bacteria, peptidoglycan represents about ___ of the dry weight of the cell wall.
questions.explanation: Peptidoglycan is ~10% of the GN cell-wall dry weight (vs ~50% in Gram-positives).

questions.stem: Peptidoglycan may contain:
questions.explanation: Peptidoglycan = NAG-NAM backbone cross-linked by peptides containing D-amino acids (e.g. D-alanine). Dipicolinic acid is a spore component.

questions.stem: In the cytoplasmic stage of peptidoglycan biosynthesis:

options.t: The NAM-pentapeptide subunit is linked to bactoprenol
The NAG-NAM-pentapeptide subunit is incorporated into existing peptidoglycan
Cross-links are formed between the lateral peptide chains

questions.explanation: Cytoplasmic stage: synthesis of the NAM-pentapeptide (Park nucleotide). Bactoprenol linkage = membrane stage; incorporation/cross-linking = outside.

questions.stem: In the membrane stage of peptidoglycan biosynthesis:

options.t: The NAM-pentapeptide subunit is linked to bactoprenol
The NAG-NAM-pentapeptide subunit is incorporated into existing peptidoglycan
Cross-links are formed between the lateral peptide chains

questions.explanation: Membrane stage: the NAM-pentapeptide is linked to the lipid carrier bactoprenol (lipid I/II).

questions.stem: Which of the following molecules is exposed on the outer surface of a Gram-positive bacterium?

options.t: The components of the electron transport system

questions.explanation: Teichoic/lipoteichoic acids are exposed on Gram-positive surfaces; LPS and Braun's lipoprotein belong to Gram-negatives.

questions.stem: Which statement about the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria is correct?

options.t: It contains teichoic acids (TA) and lipoteichoic acid (LTA)
It is impermeable to all molecules
It is permeable to all molecules thanks to porins

questions.explanation: The GN outer membrane contains LPS/LOS; porins allow selective (not unrestricted) permeability.

questions.stem: Where are the porins of Gram-negative bacteria located?
questions.explanation: Porins are channel proteins of the outer membrane.

questions.stem: If FtsZ is inactivated in Escherichia coli:

options.t: The bacteria become filamentous because they cannot divide
The bacteria become round because they cannot elongate

questions.explanation: FtsZ forms the division (Z) ring; without it cells elongate but cannot divide → filaments.

questions.stem: If MreB is inactivated in Escherichia coli:

options.t: The bacteria become round because they cannot elongate
The bacteria become filamentous because they cannot divide

questions.explanation: MreB directs lateral (rod) elongation; losing it makes rod-shaped cells round.

questions.stem: Which of the following is directly involved in energy production in bacterial cells?
questions.explanation: Bacteria lack mitochondria; the cytoplasmic membrane hosts the electron transport chain / oxidative phosphorylation.

questions.stem: Which statements about the replication of bacterial DNA are correct?

options.t: It involves the synthesis of a complementary RNA strand
It requires the RNA polymerase enzyme
It happens only during cell division

questions.explanation: Bacterial DNA replication occurs in the cytoplasm (no nucleus) and uses DNA polymerase, not RNA polymerase.

questions.stem: How many ORFs does a bacterial genome of 2.5 megabases (Mb) approximately encode?

options.t: It is not possible to estimate the number of ORFs

questions.explanation: Roughly ~1 gene per kb → a 2.5 Mb genome encodes ~2000 ORFs.

questions.stem: In genetic exchange between bacteria, what is conjugation?

options.t: A mechanism through which different cells transfer genetic material by means of physical contact
A mechanism by which cells take up DNA present in soluble form in the environment
A mechanism that allows multiplication of the bacterial cell

questions.explanation: Conjugation = transfer of genetic material via direct cell-to-cell contact (sex pilus).

questions.stem: In genetic exchange between bacteria, what is transformation?

options.t: Transfer of genetic material via physical contact between cells
Uptake of DNA present in soluble form in the environment
A mechanism that allows multiplication of the bacterial cell

questions.explanation: Transformation = uptake of free (soluble) DNA from the environment.

questions.stem: Plasmid DNA:

options.t: Can be transmitted to other bacteria
Is essential for survival under all conditions

questions.explanation: Plasmids are extrachromosomal, self-replicating, and transmissible (e.g. by conjugation); they are usually dispensable.

questions.stem: What could be the phenotypic consequence of a point mutation in a bacterial gene encoding a protein?

options.t: The protein is produced in a truncated form if the mutation creates an early stop codon
The protein is produced with altered functionality if there is a missense mutation
The protein is produced normally if the mutation does not change any amino acid

questions.explanation: Nonsense → truncation; missense → altered function; silent → no change. Point mutations are not always lethal.

questions.stem: A selective medium is used to:

options.t: Select the most effective antibiotics on the microorganisms under study
Selectively allow the growth of certain microbial species over others

questions.explanation: Selective media favour growth of target species while inhibiting others.

questions.stem: Which statement does NOT correctly describe a selective medium for bacterial growth?

options.t: It contains agents that promote growth of certain bacteria by inhibiting others
It may contain antibiotics, other substances or dyes to limit unwanted microorganisms
It is used to isolate specific bacterial species from a mixed sample
It promotes the indiscriminate growth of all types of bacteria present in a sample

questions.explanation: A selective medium does the opposite of promoting indiscriminate growth — that statement is the incorrect one.

questions.stem: The transport medium is:

options.t: A medium that keeps the organisms alive, prevents their deterioration, and inhibits replication
A medium that promotes rapid bacterial growth
A medium used to select effective antibiotics
A medium that kills contaminating organisms

questions.explanation: Transport media maintain viability without allowing multiplication during sample transport.

questions.stem: The resident microbial flora interferes with colonization of body surfaces by pathogens through which mechanisms?

options.t: Competition for nutrients and production of bacteriocins

questions.explanation: Normal flora competes for nutrients/receptors, produces bacteriocins and toxic metabolites; it does not itself make lysozyme or antibodies.

questions.stem: In which of the following body sites/organs is resident microbial flora present?
questions.explanation: Oral cavity and large intestine are heavily colonized; the lung and bladder are normally (near-)sterile.

questions.stem: Which of the following regions/organs does NOT have resident microbial flora?
questions.explanation: The lung is normally free of a resident flora; oral cavity, intestine and skin are colonized.

questions.stem: Opportunistic bacteria are defined as such if:

options.t: They always and in every case cause disease
They increase the ability of other microbes to cause disease
They cause disease only under particular conditions

questions.explanation: Opportunistic (conditional) pathogens cause disease only under predisposing conditions.

questions.stem: Bacterial virulence:

options.t: Is the relative ability of a given bacterial species to cause harm
Reflects the amount of harm caused by a given bacterial species
Is an intrinsic, unmodifiable characteristic of all pathogenic species

questions.explanation: Virulence is the degree of pathogenicity, can be quantified (ID50/LD50), and varies/can be modified — it is not a fixed intrinsic trait.

questions.stem: Which of the following extracellular enzymes of bacterial origin have a role in the spread and invasive capacity of pathogens?
questions.explanation: Collagenase and hyaluronidase degrade extracellular matrix → spread. Muramidase/catalase are not spreading factors.

questions.stem: Which bacterial structure plays a direct role in motility and spread?
questions.explanation: The flagellum provides motility; fimbriae mediate adhesion, not motility.

questions.stem: Bacterial fimbriae:

options.t: Serve for the adhesion of bacteria to different surfaces
Are the main organelle of locomotion

questions.explanation: Fimbriae/pili are proteinaceous adhesion structures.

questions.stem: The bacterial capsule:

options.t: Is mainly made of polysaccharides (with the exception of protein capsules)

questions.explanation: The capsule is usually polysaccharide (some are polypeptide) and is antiphagocytic; it is not peptidoglycan and is dispensable for growth.

questions.stem: Which of the following best represents the sequence of bacterial biofilm formation?

options.t: Reversible attachment → irreversible attachment → matrix formation and growth → dispersion
Matrix formation and growth → irreversible attachment → reversible attachment → dispersion
Irreversible attachment → dispersion → reversible attachment → matrix formation
Dispersion → matrix formation → reversible attachment → irreversible attachment

questions.explanation: Biofilm: reversible attachment → irreversible attachment → maturation (matrix + growth) → dispersion.

questions.stem: Biofilms:

options.t: Can develop on materials such as plastic, metal, and rubber
Can be composed of only a single type of bacteria
Are exclusively formed by Gram-positive bacteria

questions.explanation: Biofilms form on abiotic surfaces (plastic, metal, rubber), are often polymicrobial and are difficult to remove.

questions.stem: Obligately anaerobic bacteria are:

options.t: Able to respire in the presence of O₂
Unable to respire in the presence of O₂ and are killed by exposure to O₂

questions.explanation: Obligate anaerobes cannot use O₂ and are typically killed by it (lack catalase/SOD).

questions.stem: Which statement about anaerobic bacteria is correct?

options.t: They are able to survive and grow in environments without oxygen
They must always be treated with anaerobe-specific antibiotics

questions.explanation: Anaerobes grow in the absence of oxygen.

questions.stem: The lack of enzymes such as catalase and superoxide dismutase can explain why some bacteria:

options.t: Are killed by exposure to O₂

questions.explanation: Without catalase/SOD, toxic reactive oxygen species accumulate → cells are killed by O₂.

questions.stem: Which statement about bacterial growth is correct?

options.t: The stationary phase is characterized by a balance between cell growth and cell death
The lag phase is the one in which bacteria divide most rapidly
During the exponential phase, bacteria adapt to the new environment
The decline phase is the one in which cell division is at its maximum

questions.explanation: In stationary phase growth and death roughly balance; rapid division occurs in the exponential phase.

questions.stem: A bacterium that divides every 30 minutes starts at 200 CFU/mL. Assuming it stays in the logarithmic phase, what is the population after 3 hours?
questions.explanation: 3 h / 30 min = 6 doublings → 200 × 2⁶ = 200 × 64 = 12 800 CFU/mL.

questions.stem: A bacterium capable of growing at 45 °C, with an optimal growth temperature of 37 °C, that cannot grow below 25 °C is:
questions.explanation: Optimum ~37 °C with range ~25–45 °C is a mesophile (typical of warm-blooded-host bacteria).

questions.stem: Which statement about bacterial fermentation is correct?

options.t: It produces ATP only at the substrate level, in the absence of an external electron acceptor
It requires oxygen as the final electron acceptor
It produces a high amount of ATP
It is an exclusive process of Gram-positive bacteria

questions.explanation: Fermentation is substrate-level phosphorylation only, with no external electron acceptor, giving little ATP.

questions.stem: Which of the following procedures are able to kill spores?
questions.explanation: Autoclaving (moist heat under pressure) and incineration are sporicidal; pasteurization and cold are not.

questions.stem: Which of the following methods can be used to sterilize a heat-sensitive liquid?
questions.explanation: Heat-sensitive liquids are sterilized by filtration (e.g. 0.22 µm membrane).

questions.stem: Compared with the vegetative bacterial cell, the spore has:

options.t: High resistance to heat and drying

questions.explanation: Endospores are metabolically dormant and highly resistant to heat, desiccation and chemicals.

questions.stem: Which of the following microorganisms are spore-forming?
questions.explanation: Endospore-formers are mainly Bacillus and Clostridium/Clostridioides.

questions.stem: Which of the following microorganisms cannot be cultured in vitro?
questions.explanation: T. pallidum and M. leprae cannot be grown on artificial media.

questions.stem: An antimicrobial agent that could be used as an antibiotic should:

options.t: Have a limited spectrum of activity
Be able to be administered in high doses
Be administered only via the parenteral route

questions.explanation: Selective toxicity (harming the microbe but not the host) is the key requirement.

questions.stem: Which statement about antibiotics is correct?

options.t: They are substances produced by microorganisms that act against bacteria
They can be used to treat viral infections
They are also effective against viruses

questions.explanation: Antibiotics are (originally) microbial products active against bacteria; they do not work on viruses and can select resistance.

questions.stem: Which genera of microorganisms are known as major producers of antibiotics?
questions.explanation: Penicillium (fungus) and Streptomyces (actinomycete) are the classic antibiotic producers.

questions.stem: Which of these antibacterial drugs inhibit the synthesis of the cell wall?
questions.explanation: β-lactams (amoxicillin) and glycopeptides (vancomycin) target cell-wall synthesis; fluoroquinolones target DNA gyrase.

questions.stem: Which of these antibacterial drugs inhibits DNA synthesis/replication?
questions.explanation: Ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) inhibits DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV → DNA replication. Rifampicin targets RNA polymerase.

questions.stem: What is the target of levofloxacin / fluoroquinolones?

options.t: DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV (DNA replication)

questions.explanation: Fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV.

questions.stem: What is the target of teicoplanin?

options.t: The synthesis of the cell wall

questions.explanation: Teicoplanin is a glycopeptide → inhibits cell-wall synthesis (binds D-Ala-D-Ala).

questions.stem: Which of these antibiotics acts against the cell membrane?
questions.explanation: Daptomycin (a lipopeptide) disrupts the bacterial cell membrane.

questions.stem: Which antibiotic blocks protein synthesis?
questions.explanation: Streptomycin (aminoglycoside) binds the 30S subunit → blocks protein synthesis.

questions.stem: Which of these antibiotics blocks the synthesis of folic acid?
questions.explanation: Sulfonamides (with trimethoprim) inhibit folate synthesis.

questions.stem: Characteristics of fluoroquinolone antibiotics include:

options.t: Inhibition of the bacterial cell wall

questions.explanation: Fluoroquinolones are broad-spectrum, concentration-dependent bactericidal DNA-gyrase inhibitors.

questions.stem: Which microorganisms are naturally resistant to the action of β-lactams?
questions.explanation: Mycoplasma/Ureaplasma lack a cell wall (no peptidoglycan target) → intrinsic β-lactam resistance.

questions.stem: Enterobacteriaceae are naturally resistant to the action of vancomycin. Why?

options.t: They do not have the biochemical target
They produce only low-affinity PBPs for β-lactams

questions.explanation: Vancomycin is simply too large to cross the Gram-negative outer membrane to reach its (present) target — so 'none of the above'.

questions.stem: Which statement about bacterial resistance to antibiotics is correct?

options.t: It can be acquired only through gene mutation
It can be acquired both through mutation and through genetic exchange
It concerns only pathogenic bacteria in humans

questions.explanation: Resistance arises by mutation AND horizontal gene transfer, and can also be intrinsic (natural).

questions.stem: Which statement about the MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration) or MBC (minimum bactericidal concentration) is correct?

options.t: The MIC inhibits the growth of the bacterium, whereas the MBC kills 99.9% of the bacteria
The MIC eliminates bacteria, whereas the MBC inhibits growth
The MIC and the MBC are always the same for a given antibiotic
MIC and MBC have no correlation with therapeutic efficacy

questions.explanation: MIC = lowest conc. that inhibits growth; MBC = lowest conc. that kills ≥99.9%.

questions.stem: What does the parameter 'ECOFF' represent in EUCAST antibiotic susceptibility testing?

options.t: The breakpoint between 'wild-type' and 'non-wild-type' for a species, based on the MIC
The minimum effective dose of an antibiotic
The maximum tolerable concentration of an antibiotic in the blood
The probability of developing side effects during treatment

questions.explanation: The ECOFF (epidemiological cut-off) separates wild-type from non-wild-type (acquired-resistance) populations by MIC.

questions.stem: Which part of the endotoxin is linked to its toxic activity?
questions.explanation: Lipid A is the toxic moiety of LPS/LOS endotoxin.

questions.stem: Koch's postulates require that:

options.t: The suspected pathogen is present in all cases of the disease
The organism can be grown in pure culture
The cultured organism reproduces the disease when inoculated into a healthy host
The organism must be visible by Gram stain

questions.explanation: Koch's postulates: presence in disease, pure culture, reproduction of disease, re-isolation. Gram-stainability is not a postulate.
